author: @bikerfirefarter7280
text:
Back in the day/college, we pyrolysed several hydrocarbons (polythene, diesel, petroleum jelly, paraffin wax etc) but we added a pinch of wood-ash as a catalyst, the products of H2 and Alkenes were obtained at lower temperatures than without the ash. I suspect several metal oxides (rust, Aluminium oxide etc) should also catalyse, but I haven't tested that. (I don't remember if we did it with vegetable/animal oils/fats; it might be worth looking into).
However, once the reaction starts, some of the gasses could be fed to a burner under the chamber to maintain the process, the remainder gasses/products being tapped off for storage or other uses.
A similar re-circulator method is used to pyrolise wood to charcoal in a double-walled oil-drum retort, starting a fire between the inner/outer drums with a small amount of scrap/sacrificial wood starts pyrolysing the contents of the inner chamber (which is pierced underneath) those gasses then burn under the inner chamber and also inbetween the residual charcoal from the starter/sacrificial wood and the reaction becomes self sustaining. Once started the process needs no baby-sitting and burns cleanly while you do something else. The charcoal obtained is very high quality, which lights easily due to residual carbon products. I use the charcoal for BBQs, in my forge, for heating, or grind it up and use it as a soil improver on my allotment (being careful to only use clean/untreated timber for the latter as some treatments use copper, chrome and arsenic).

author: @oldschoolcfi3833
text: Vehicles have been converted to use this "Woodgas" when oil was unavailable, such as WWII, or when prices where prohibitively high, like in the 70s.   Today's cars probably couldn't support the conversion, but for an older vehicle it was fairly simple, a pyrolysis chamber was bolted on, usually in the boot, or externally, and simply plumbed into the carburetor.  Some more complex systems allowed for starting on petrol, and switching over to woodgas as the system began to heat up, allowing for faster starting.   The power of woodgas is somewhat less than liquid fuels, but the 'crude' stock is readily available.

author: @walterrutherford8321
text:
Reminds me of a very simple experiment we did for an organic chemistry class. Put sawdust in a test tube and heat it over a Bunsen burner. The tube was connected to a pipette and the gases produced burned easily. The professor called what happened to the wood “charking”, which is different than burning since the test tube contained little oxygen. I’ve seen a vehicle being run off those gases in a wood-burning truck.
Since you use a lot of power to produce the gas I wondered about the usefulness of this process. One possible benefit would be converting wood to a different burnable produce, charcoal, that can be stored without worrying about it rotting, molding, or attracting termites or nesting pests. And if you heat a structure with the pyrolysis flame you can compress and store the gases as a byproduct for later use in existing (slightly modified) gas appliances: water heater, gas stove, etc. Two birds, one stone. The benefit there is the gas lights immediately with no warmup time and doesn’t generate much extra unneeded heat like a woodstove.

author: @johnparsons9620
text:
Instead of the external heat to power pyrolysis why not use GPCS (Gas Produced Combustion System).  Feed restricted air and steam into the preheated wood or biomass waste.  This will gasifying at a lower temp than pyrolysis.  Then have your burner heat water to produce steam that is fed into the gasification.

The process becomes self sustaining until the fuel runs out.  You will need a much larger paint can.

replies.author: @consaka1
replies.text: You are talking about a catalyst. I've never heard of one that would work simply because it would need to get hot. Heat is what causes the wood to break down into gas. As for using the syngas to do the actual heating that should be possible but you would need to prevent heat loss in the wood chamber for efficiency reasons and you would have to jump start it with a lot of heat. This is done all the time with oil burners.

replies.author: @arebuffat
replies.text: @consaka1 it is stated from a reserach study that adding 1 to 3 % of common ash might improve pyrolysis. Is ash acting as a catalyser? Well I don’t know but somewhere in the web there is an answer with the scientific référence.

replies.author: @Noswiatel
replies.text: Woodgas doesnt compress that well, syngas does it a little better but still disappointing with small compressors. You can store it in evacuated balloons, innertubes, yoga balls, etc. And then compress it. Be careful with a propane tank and flush it a couple of times with eater before any cuts.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
Nighthawklnlight built a gasometer that you might like so search for and watch. This may store many of the non-condensables. 
 

Will syngas condense under pressure? Hydrogen and carbon monoxide, major energy carrying components of syngas, only condense under very cold temperatures.

 Pyrolysis is also likely to produce other products that will condense out at room temperature and or under pressure, and could be stored separately as a condensed fuel.

author: @jamest.5001
text:
I like to imagine I need to fuel a generator from what I have at home, long term,  I can imagine starting with concentrated sun heating wood chips to start with, catch the methanol, while the generator is running on the vapors, like syngas, and possibly convert the gas engine to run on oil fuels like a hot bulb diesel,  I have seen small gas engines converted to diesel, (not sure how long it would last) using small diesel engine parts, I think I'd  mostly use paper garbage convert it to methanol, use that as fuel in the gas engine,  converting a carburator engine to run on methanol is not difficult, basically just adjusting the carb, and larger jet,  in some carbs just remove the jet, easy especially in steady speed engines like a generator,  getting it to idle may not be so easy,  even running the engine with a partial choke, to get more fuel from the carb, adjusting the idle circuit to add fuel, while at speed, is possible, since the idle isn't needed,  the main thing is getting it to run under load, at the proper speed for 50-60HZ depending on where you live, I'm in the strange. World where. The loads in a home are put in series,  at the breaker panel and the imbalance is sent through the neutral wire to the transformer, if you have two 100w loads, the neutral has no current, add one 100w load, and the neutral has 100w as long as both lines or legs are even, the neutral has nothing on it ( from the breaker box to transformer not inside the home)  so it's basically a complex way of putting two 120v lamps in series powering it with 240v,  I just wonder who thought this up, and how did they convince everyone this is the way to do it? I'd like to hear that meeting!! 🙃 
Why not power things with 480v at 60kHz  over 120/240  60 Hz, or why not 240/480 at 20-30hz, or 60/120 v and 120hz or 240Hz  , I see they had issues insulating from high voltage in the late 1800's  sorry I'm rambling again,

author: @TrumpstienPhile
text:
I wated to reuse waste oil to generate soot/smoke free heat. I tried passing 15kv@ 100w(from an electronic neon sign transformer) through  oil(olive because  its what i had) to pyrolyse the oil- initially it didn't work(oil dielectric  strength too high) but then I added a bit of copper dust(to make it conductive and the oil started bubling rapidly. The gas which was produced popped under flame(high hydrogen content?)

According to google a litre of oil generates 11kw of heat  if you could gassify it with only  100 watts that would be  amazing 

Obviously  15ma at 15000 volts will be LETHAL so don't try it at home unless you absolutely  know what you are doing and take tons of precautions

author: @jb-dn3ct
text: Trying to get my head around what was mentioned about pyrolysis being something heated without oxygen, or more the effects when compared to being heated in a kiln, I'm  presuming that the sealed nature of pyrolysis allows for a sort of control in capturing what the biomass is releasing where as in a kiln it would just be venting to atmosphere, or is there more to it? Would pyrolysis have the same result on what you did with the carbon mushrooms? I haven't got a kiln but if the mushrooms maintained there shape through pyrolysis then I'd like to try compressing a load of mushrooms together into a usable shape when reduced to carbon to try as a wick, I understand it would be brittle but im thinking the porosity of the mushrooms structure  should make a great wick, of course this all depends on them maintaining there shape like they did in the kiln

author: @Absfor30
text: Robert, if you pipe the gas into an upturned vessel filled with water you can collect the gas instead of burning it at source... The gas will replace the liquid in the vessel and then you can use it wherever else you think best.

replies.text: cheers mate

author: @drakinfire7205
text: Pyrolysis of vegetable oil is a good one to get a diesel like liquid it caches fire a lot better than the vegetable oil. could be a alternate way to make biodiesel no need for a alkaline or alcohol. i used a solar concentrator with my set up.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: interesting idea mate - cheers

replies.author: @drakinfire7205
replies.text:
this could be helpful 
Palm oil is fully converted by pyrolysis at 420C both with and without catalysts.
Thermal conversion produces big amounts of saponifiables as fatty acids.
The basic catalyst MgO–Al2O3 kills saponifiables, but produces heavy ketones.
Lewis acid catalysts as alumina produce more hydrocarbons.
Brønsted acidic H-ZSM-5 zeolite kills olefins producing more paraffins and aromatics.

replies.author: @consaka1
replies.text:
Methanol is a primary ingredient in making biodiesel as is good old lye. Methanol is some nasty toxic stuff that burns clean. Lye is pretty nasty stuff too. Old used cooking oil is considered hazardous waste. Mix the lye and methanol together and you get something, methoxide if I recall, really nasty that will eat your skin off. Mix that with the used vegetable oil and mix it really good and you get glycerin and biodiesel. Both of which are relatively harmless. Glycerin is used in makeup and other skin creams. I've seen pictures of guys drinking biodiesel though I wouldn't recommend it.
Yeah I used to make it.

author: @richbuilds_com
text:
I good demonstration of the process, but it begs the question: Are you putting in more energy at the bottom than you are getting out of the top?

Genuine question, as I have no idea of the energy outputs of either end, but the gas canister 'feels' more energy expensive.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: in this demo we are putting more energy because we are just driving off the water and gas from a few twigs to show the idea in but in general it is the other way round and very often the heat of burning is used to run the paralysis and gasification stages - so you get fire, heat and extra gas

replies.author: @richbuilds_com
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering Awesome - thanks for the clarification.
